text: RAG combines a pre-trained sequence-to-sequence model with a dense retriever so generated answers can condition on external passages instead of relying only on fixed model parameters.
The paper contrasts retrieving passages once for the whole sequence with retrieving per token, which frames retrieval as a central architectural decision in early RAG systems.
RAG improves knowledge-intensive task performance while producing more specific and factual responses than a purely parametric baseline, but it still depends heavily on retriever quality.
Self-RAG introduces reflection tokens that let the model decide whether to retrieve, how to use evidence, and when to critique the faithfulness and utility of its own answer.
Rather than always retrieving, the model learns to trigger retrieval only when it expects external evidence to help, which can reduce unnecessary context and support more targeted reasoning.
The paper reports stronger citation quality and answer quality by combining retrieval with explicit critique signals, at the cost of a more complex training and inference recipe.
CRAG focuses on the failure mode where retrieved documents are low quality and proposes a retrieval evaluator that decides whether evidence is reliable enough to pass downstream.
When retrieval is weak, CRAG augments or corrects the evidence set before generation and also decomposes long documents into smaller knowledge units for refinement.
CRAG is especially compelling when the application risk comes from noisy retrieval because it adds an explicit quality-control layer instead of trusting the retriever output blindly.
